headline: Riley is learning fast from docs, but key decision/ownership context is still missing—so he’s holding questions back until incidents force clarity.
summary_bullets: Riley can trace request paths, but he lacks the historical tradeoff context behind decisions.
Ownership boundaries are fuzzy when priorities conflict, and he avoids interrupting by parking questions in a notes doc.
He wants a concrete escalation/incident calibration path (what to escalate vs batch) and a weekly senior architecture review.
understanding_paragraph: Riley is landing in the codebase quickly, but the session revealed a gap between technical understanding and how decisions and ownership actually work day-to-day. He’s actively trying to be respectful (“trying not to interrupt people”), which turns into delayed questions and reliance on incident moments for handoff details. The most actionable need is a clear escalation/ownership map plus a lightweight senior feedback loop to calibrate architecture assumptions.

axes.meaning: Weak signal, not actionable on its own; he reports comfort with reading services and steadier next steps.
A real friction pattern: he’s investing effort but holding questions back, which reduces ownership confidence and initiative.
The defining signal: decision-making, domain ownership, and escalation lines are unclear enough to change how he asks for help.
Worth noting and acting on: he needs faster calibration via a senior review loop to avoid architecture assumptions.

brutal_truth_employee: Riley said, “I am less sure how decisions are really made here,” and “I keep questions in a notes doc instead of asking.” That means his current onboarding loop is too dependent on live incidents to surface the missing handoff context.
brutal_truth_manager: When Riley offered a concrete mechanism—“A short weekly architecture review with someone senior would help me calibrate faster”—the next step should have been to lock the cadence and the owner, not just acknowledge the need. The moment to deepen was his escalation/ownership confusion (“who is on point for each domain when priorities conflict”); that’s the thread to turn into a specific escalation map and named contacts.

next_actions.action: Set up a 30-minute weekly architecture review for Riley with a named senior; confirm the first session time and the agenda template.
Create and share a one-page escalation guide for Riley: what to escalate vs batch, who to page, and what “ownership” means during priority conflicts.
Loop back on Riley’s notes-doc habit: ask what he asked live in the last incident review and what still blocked him from asking earlier.

watch_for: Within two weeks: if Riley still stores questions in a notes doc instead of asking in real time, the ownership/escalation map isn’t landing.
Before next 1:1: if the weekly senior architecture review doesn’t happen on schedule, Riley will keep calibrating only during incidents.
